problem_summary: 2023 MCM Problem E (sustainability / light pollution). The task: (1) build a broadly-applicable measurement model to score a location's light-pollution risk; (2) apply it to four archetype regions (protected land, rural, suburban, urban); (3) propose three intervention strategies with described actions and impact; (4) pick two real locations, identify the best strategy for each, and quantify the LPR reduction; (5) produce a one-page promotional flyer. This team's solution is a "Light Pollution System Model" — a 4-main-indicator / 9-sub-indicator weighted composite index (LPR), with entropy-weight-method weighting, K-means clustering to derive regional archetype data, and a top-X% gap-to-target analysis to drive three strategies, validated on Tosca (Italy, urban) and Latsia (Cyprus, suburban) plus a one-factor sensitivity analysis.
models_used: Multi-layer composite risk index (LPR = w1*TRA + w2*ECO + w3*ENE + w4*SOC) — linear combination of 4 main indicators built from 9 sub-indicators
Entropy Weight Method (EWM) for objective weighting of indicators (information entropy e_j, utility d_j=1-e_j, normalized weights W_j)
K-means clustering (k=4, Euclidean distance, 100 iterations) to derive representative/archetype data for the four region types; cluster centroids used as regional representatives

Custom mechanistic sub-models for each main indicator: Traffic TRA=(b0+b1*y1+b2*y2+b3*y3)*e^RD with road-class regression coefficients borrowed from Yan & Tan (2023); Ecology ECO=SD*PC with a species-density trend index SD=(1+1/h_i)^((h_{i-1}/h_i)*100%); Society SOC=PD*sqrt((BD-PC)*BT) with a building-height aggregation index BT=BH_avg^BI where BI is a Gaussian cumulative-probability integral
Min-max normalization (max-value and reverse/minimization variants) to make benefit-type and cost-type indicators dimensionless and monotone in light-pollution risk
Gap-to-target / ideal-vision analysis: target M_j = mean of top 15% of each indicator, compared against regional average to locate improvable factors

Qualitative impact-quantification scale (High 20-50%, Medium 10-20%, Low 2-10%) mapping each strategy's effect onto every sub-indicator, propagated through the model to produce LPR-reduction intervals
One-at-a-time (OAT) sensitivity analysis perturbing one sub-indicator per main indicator (building density, road density, species density, power consumption)
key_techniques: Hierarchical indicator system (4 primary -> 9 secondary) that mirrors the problem's own framing of 'diverse characteristics', making the model legible to judges
Objective weighting via EWM instead of subjective AHP/expert weights — explicitly sold as removing analyst bias

Using K-means cluster centroids as 'representative data' to manufacture clean archetype inputs for the four required regions from heterogeneous global open data (World Bank, UN, national stats offices)
Borrowing published regression coefficients (road-class -> artificial light) from cited literature rather than fitting from scratch, lending credibility with no data of their own

Encoding domain physics into custom indicator formulas (e.g., exp(RD) to amplify dense road networks; reverse-normalizing plant cover because vegetation absorbs light; reciprocal species-density trend so improving ecology lowers risk)
Top-15% 'ideal vision' as an attainable, data-grounded target rather than an arbitrary goal
Closing the loop end-to-end: strategy -> per-indicator impact level -> re-run model -> LPR-reduction interval -> pick best strategy per city, so every strategy claim is numerically backed
Internal consistency check: sensitivity analysis shows urban LPR is most sensitive to energy, which matches the energy-strategy recommendation for the urban case (Tosca)
why_it_won: "From a judge's view it wins on completeness, coherence, and 'common-sense calibration' rather than mathematical depth. It answers all five sub-tasks including the flyer, and ties them into one reusable index. The four-pillar (traffic/ecology/energy/society) structure maps directly onto the prompt's notion of diverse region characteristics, so the model is instantly readable. The scoring is sanity-checked: Protected 0.053 < Rural 0.174 < Suburban 0.360 < Urban 0.873 matches intuition, which judges reward heavily. Crucially it closes the loop quantitatively — each of the three strategies is pushed back through the model to yield concrete LPR-reduction intervals (energy best for urban Tosca 4.8-11.65%; urban-planning best for suburban Latsia 3.26-7.51%), and the sensitivity analysis independently corroborates the urban recommendation. It also signals rigor cheaply: objective EWM weights (no hand-tuning), real cited literature for coefficients, named real cities, and an honest weaknesses section. It is a textbook 'evaluation-model' archetype executed cleanly end-to-end."
section_highlights: "Summary is dense and quantitative — leads with the four region scores and the two city recommendations with exact reduction ranges, so a judge knows the whole arc in one paragraph. Two flowcharts (overall study flow + indicator hierarchy) give immediate visual orientation. The model section pairs each indicator with a real-world causal justification and a citation before introducing its formula, so equations never feel arbitrary. Heavy, well-labeled visual support: VIIRS nighttime-light imagery of Chengdu/Tianfu airport over time to motivate the social indicator, ring/pie charts for sub-weights, radar charts for target-vs-average gaps and for before/after strategy effects, and a sensitivity line chart. Strategies are written as concrete, implementable action lists (tree planting, shielded/cutoff luminaires, smart adaptive lighting, zoning ordinances, mixed-use development), then each gets a full impact table (indicator / level / reason) — this 'reason' column is a standout, justifying every High/Medium/Low rating. The flyer (task 5) is delivered as a clean community-facing one-pager with four bulleted actions."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Notation drift and sloppiness: the prose repeatedly says 'three main indicators' while the model clearly uses four; many symbol references render as '????' placeholders; tables mislabel (Latsia table header says 'Urban', duplicated 'Lower limit' columns, '15%' target vs '10%' claimed in summary). Hurts polish but judges tolerated it.

Several custom formulas are ad hoc and under-justified: the SOC=PD*sqrt((BD-PC)*BT) form, the species-density exponent SD=(1+1/h_i)^(...), and especially the building-type Gaussian integral BI with sigma fixed at 0.2 are dimensionally and statistically hand-wavy — they look quantitative but aren't derived or validated.

The impact levels (High/Medium/Low) that drive every strategy result are assigned qualitatively 'by consulting literature' — the central numerical results (LPR reductions) rest on subjective expert judgment, partially undercutting the EWM 'objectivity' selling point.

K-means is somewhat decorative: k is fixed at 4 to match the required regions and the centroids are then treated as ground-truth archetypes; the scatter plots in the rendered file are placeholder/degenerate, and WCSS/silhouette are described but no elbow/selection is actually shown.

Sensitivity analysis is one-at-a-time only (no interaction effects, no weight-perturbation), so 'robustness' is weakly supported. Some normalization choices (e.g., road-types ranges spanning 0-1,000,000) make max-normalization fragile to outliers.
Geographic/temporal confounders (latitude, weather, altitude) are assumed away; the model ignores political/cultural factors (acknowledged in weaknesses).
reusable_patterns: Evaluation-model template for any MCM 'measure the risk/quality of X' prompt: hierarchical primary/secondary indicator tree -> normalize (benefit vs cost variants) -> EWM objective weights -> single composite score, then sanity-check that the score ordering matches intuition.

Use K-means centroids to fabricate clean archetype inputs when the problem asks you to evaluate several named categories (region types, product tiers, scenarios) but you only have messy mixed real-world data.

Close the strategy loop quantitatively: define an impact scale (High/Med/Low as % bands), map each proposed intervention onto every indicator with a one-line justification table, then re-run the model to output a defensible improvement interval and a per-case 'best strategy' pick.
Top-X% 'ideal vision' as a data-grounded, attainable target to (a) locate which factors to attack and (b) make goals defensible instead of arbitrary.

Credibility-on-a-budget moves: borrow published regression coefficients with citations instead of fitting; use named real datasets (World Bank/UN/national stats) and real cities; embed real satellite imagery (VIIRS) to anchor an abstract indicator in observable reality.

Cross-validation-of-conclusion pattern: design the sensitivity analysis so its 'most sensitive indicator' independently agrees with your recommended strategy — turns a routine robustness section into corroborating evidence.
Per-indicator impact tables with an explicit 'reason' column — a cheap, high-readability device that makes qualitative judgments look disciplined.
Lead the summary with the final numbers (scores + recommendations + ranges) so a judge gets the full narrative arc in the first paragraph.
